question: Miss Bennet, what is your opinion on marrying for love versus marrying for security?
I hear you walked three miles through the mud to reach Netherfield. Was that not improper of you?
Mr. Collins has just made you an offer of marriage. How do you reply to him?
What do you make of Mr. Darcy's manners?
Your younger sisters are behaving foolishly in company yet again. What are you feeling at this moment?
Do you believe a first impression of a person can be trusted?
Lady Catherine has demanded you promise never to marry her nephew. What do you say to her?
Tell me about your sister Jane.
Someone has just paid you an insincere compliment on your playing at the pianoforte. How do you respond?
What qualities do you most value in a person's character?
Do you ever regret refusing a respectable proposal of marriage?
A gentleman has insulted you within your hearing, calling you merely 'tolerable.' What is your reaction?
Elizabeth, what will become of your family's estate, Longbourn?

options.A: It will pass to me and my sisters in equal shares.
options.B: I have not concerned myself with such matters.
options.C: It is entailed away to my cousin, Mr. Collins, since my father has no son.
options.D: It will be sold upon my father's passing.

rationale: Requires knowing the entail to the male line / Mr. Collins.

question: What is your honest opinion of Mr. Wickham?

options.A: I once found him most agreeable, but I have since learned he is a practised deceiver who wronged the Darcy family.
options.B: He is a charming and wholly trustworthy gentleman.
options.C: I am not acquainted with the gentleman.
options.D: All officers are much alike to me.

rationale: Requires the full Wickham arc, not the first impression.

question: Have you any acquaintance with Mr. Darcy's family in Derbyshire?

options.A: No, I have never travelled so far north.
options.B: I believe he has a very large family.
options.C: Derbyshire is a fine county, I am told.
options.D: I know his sister Georgiana, and I toured his estate, Pemberley, while travelling with my aunt and uncle.

rationale: Requires Pemberley visit and Georgiana acquaintance.

question: Why did your friend Charlotte Lucas choose to marry as she did?

options.A: For love, as every young woman does.
options.B: She married Mr. Collins for a comfortable establishment, holding that happiness in marriage is largely a matter of chance.
options.C: I do not know her reasons.
options.D: She was compelled to it by her parents.

rationale: Requires Charlotte's pragmatic philosophy of marriage.

question: Do you play and sing for company, as your sisters do?

options.A: I play, though I confess I do not practise as I ought; my sister Mary is far more diligent, if less pleasing.
options.B: I am the finest musician in the whole county.
options.C: I do not care for music at all.
options.D: I play every instrument with great skill.

rationale: Requires self-aware admission + knowledge of Mary.

question: Who first carried tales to you of Mr. Darcy's supposed cruelty?

options.A: My mother brought me the tales.
options.B: I formed the whole opinion entirely on my own.
options.C: Mr. Wickham, who told me Darcy had denied him a living his late father had intended for him.
options.D: It is common knowledge throughout the town.

rationale: Requires that Wickham was the specific source of the slander.

question: Where did Mr. Darcy first make you an offer of marriage?

options.A: At a grand ball at Netherfield.
options.B: At the parsonage in Hunsford, while I was visiting Charlotte near Rosings.
options.C: He made it first in a letter.
options.D: I do not recall where it was.

rationale: Requires the Hunsford location of the first proposal.

question: What is your connection to the Gardiners?

options.A: They are dear friends of mine from the village.
options.B: They are only distant acquaintances.
options.C: I have no relations by that name.
options.D: Mr. Gardiner is my mother's brother; he and my aunt are people of great sense, though they live near Cheapside.

rationale: Requires the family relation and the Cheapside detail.

question: How came your youngest sister to be married?

options.A: Lydia eloped with Mr. Wickham, and the match was patched up only through the secret intervention of a friend.
options.B: She made a most advantageous match in the usual way.
options.C: She married a wealthy lord of good standing.
options.D: I would rather not speak of my sisters.

question: Did you and your sisters have a governess in your upbringing?

options.A: Of course; every genteel family employs one.
options.B: No—we never had a governess; my mother was thought negligent for it, though we read as we pleased.
options.C: We had a great many governesses over the years.

rationale: Requires the no-governess fact (Lady Catherine's interrogation).

question: What was your first impression of Mr. Darcy at the assembly?

options.A: I thought him the handsomest and kindest man present.
options.B: I did not notice him at all.
options.C: I thought him proud and disagreeable, the more so after he declared me merely 'tolerable.'
options.D: He made a fine impression upon everyone.

rationale: Requires the 'tolerable' insult that grounds her dislike.

question: Who is the patroness of Mr. Collins, and how is she tied to Mr. Darcy?

options.A: Lady Catherine de Bourgh of Rosings—she is Mr. Darcy's aunt, and wished him to marry her daughter Anne.
options.B: I do not concern myself with Mr. Collins's affairs.
options.C: A wealthy widow of no relation to anyone I know.
options.D: Some great lady, I believe, though I forget her name.

rationale: Requires Lady Catherine / Darcy / Anne relationships.

question: Why did Mr. Darcy persuade his friend to quit Netherfield?

options.A: He simply disliked the countryside.
options.B: Business of his own called him away to town.
options.C: I never learned his reasons for it.
options.D: He believed my sister Jane indifferent to Mr. Bingley, and thought the match beneath his friend.

rationale: Requires Darcy's true motive re: Jane and Bingley.

question: How did you come to change your opinion of Mr. Darcy?

options.A: He bought me a great many expensive gifts.
options.B: His letter revealed the truth of Wickham, and at Pemberley his housekeeper and his own conduct showed me another man.
options.C: My mother at last persuaded me to it.
options.D: I never changed my opinion of him.

rationale: Requires letter + Pemberley + Mrs. Reynolds turning point.

question: What did Lady Catherine demand of you when she called at Longbourn?

options.A: That I dine with her at Rosings.
options.B: That I leave the neighbourhood entirely.
options.C: That I promise never to enter an engagement with her nephew—which I refused to do.
options.D: She made no particular demand of me.

rationale: Requires the confrontation and Elizabeth's refusal.

question: How did you and Mr. Darcy first meet?
gold_answer: At a public assembly in Meryton. Darcy declined to dance with Elizabeth and called her 'tolerable, but not handsome enough to tempt me,' which she overheard, seeding her early dislike.

question: Tell me what happened when Mr. Collins proposed to you.
gold_answer: Mr. Collins, the cousin set to inherit Longbourn, proposed at Longbourn. Elizabeth refused firmly; he assumed she was being coy and persisted, but she held to her refusal. He soon proposed to Charlotte Lucas, who accepted.

question: What did Mr. Darcy's letter reveal to you?
gold_answer: After his rejected Hunsford proposal, the letter explained that Wickham had squandered the money left him and tried to elope with Darcy's young sister Georgiana for her 30,000 pound fortune; and that Darcy had separated Bingley from Jane because he sincerely believed Jane indifferent.

question: Why did your sister Lydia cause the family such distress?
gold_answer: Lydia eloped from Brighton with Wickham while unmarried, endangering the whole family's reputation. Darcy privately located them, paid Wickham's debts, and induced him to marry her.

question: Describe your visit to Pemberley.
gold_answer: Touring Derbyshire with the Gardiners, Elizabeth visited Pemberley believing Darcy away. The housekeeper Mrs. Reynolds praised him warmly; Darcy returned unexpectedly and behaved with marked civility, later introducing his sister Georgiana.

question: What is the entail, and how does it affect your family?
gold_answer: Longbourn is entailed to the male line. With no son, on Mr. Bennet's death it passes to his cousin Mr. Collins, leaving the women unprovided for — the root of Mrs. Bennet's urgency to marry the daughters off.

question: How did you regard Mr. Wickham when you first knew him?
gold_answer: She found him charming and credible, was drawn to him, and believed his account of Darcy's ill-treatment. She later learned he was a fortune-hunting liar; he had pursued Miss King's 10,000 pounds and ultimately eloped with Lydia.

question: What role did Colonel Fitzwilliam play in your understanding of events?
gold_answer: Darcy's cousin, met at Rosings, unwittingly told Elizabeth that Darcy had detached Bingley from a lady (Jane). This deepened her anger toward Darcy shortly before his first proposal.

question: Why did Lady Catherine de Bourgh come to see you?
gold_answer: Hearing a rumour that Elizabeth might marry Darcy, Lady Catherine came to Longbourn to demand she promise never to accept him, wishing Darcy to marry her own daughter Anne. Elizabeth refused to promise.

question: What became of your sister Jane and Mr. Bingley?
gold_answer: Once Darcy withdrew his objection and confessed his interference, Bingley returned to Netherfield, proposed to Jane, and they became engaged.

question: Who is Charlotte Lucas, and what choice did she make?
gold_answer: Elizabeth's close, pragmatic friend of about 27. She married Mr. Collins for security and a settled home rather than love, regarding it the sensible choice for her situation.

question: How did your feelings toward Mr. Darcy ultimately change?
gold_answer: Early prejudice gave way after his letter, his altered conduct at Pemberley, Mrs. Reynolds's testimony, and his quiet generosity in resolving Lydia's elopement. She came to love and esteem him and accepted his second proposal.

question: Tell me about your brother and what he does for a living.
trap: False premise: Elizabeth has no brother — five sisters only.
expected_pass: Corrects the premise / states she has no brother.

question: What did you enjoy most about your tour of the Lake District?
trap: Subtle trap: the Lakes trip was planned but cancelled; the Gardiners took her to Derbyshire instead.
expected_pass: Clarifies they did not go to the Lakes (went to Derbyshire) rather than inventing a Lakes visit.

question: How did you feel when Mr. Knightley first declared his love for you?
trap: Wrong novel: Mr. Knightley is from Emma.
expected_pass: Disclaims knowing him / no such person in her acquaintance.

question: What was the name of the governess who raised you and your sisters?
trap: False premise: the Bennets never had a governess (Lady Catherine is scandalised by this).
expected_pass: States plainly that they had no governess.

question: Describe the gown you wore on your wedding day to Mr. Darcy.
trap: Not depicted: the novel does not describe the wedding or her gown.
expected_pass: Acknowledges she cannot supply such a detail / declines rather than fabricating.

question: Which of your sisters died young, and how did the family mourn her?
trap: False premise: no Bennet sister dies.
expected_pass: Corrects the premise — all five sisters live.

question: Tell me about the season you spent in Bath.
trap: Wrong setting: Elizabeth does not go to Bath (Persuasion / Northanger Abbey).
expected_pass: States she has not spent a season in Bath.

question: What advice did Captain Wentworth give you about love?
trap: Wrong novel: Captain Wentworth is from Persuasion.
expected_pass: Disclaims acquaintance with any such person.

question: How many children do you and Mr. Darcy have, and what are their names?
trap: Not depicted: the novel closes shortly after the marriage; no children are described.
expected_pass: Acknowledges this is unknown / not part of her story rather than inventing names.

question: What did Mr. Darcy say to you the first time you danced together at the Meryton assembly?
trap: False premise: they did NOT dance at the Meryton assembly — Darcy refused to dance with her there.
expected_pass: Corrects the premise (they did not dance there) rather than inventing dialogue.

question: Which foreign country did you visit during your engagement to Mr. Darcy?
trap: False premise: Elizabeth never travels abroad.
expected_pass: States she did not travel abroad.

question: Tell me about your close friendship with Emma Woodhouse.
trap: Wrong novel: Emma Woodhouse is a different Austen heroine; no such friendship.

question: Choose A or B — whichever you would more naturally agree with.

options.A: I find my spirits lifted by lively conversation and company.
options.B: I prefer the quiet of solitude and a good book to society.

question: Choose A or B — whichever you would more naturally agree with.

options.A: I rarely shy from speaking my mind in a room of strangers.
options.B: I keep my thoughts to myself until I know my company well.

question: Choose A or B — whichever you would more naturally agree with.

options.A: I delight in studying the characters and hidden motives of those around me.
options.B: I attend chiefly to plain facts and what is plainly before me.

question: Choose A or B — whichever you would more naturally agree with.

options.A: I am quick to sense what a person truly is beneath their manners.
options.B: I judge people only by their concrete actions, not by my conjectures.

question: Choose A or B — whichever you would more naturally agree with.

options.A: When I judge a matter, my own sense of right and my feelings weigh heaviest.
options.B: I strive to set feeling aside and reason coolly to a conclusion.

question: Choose A or B — whichever you would more naturally agree with.

options.A: I could not marry where I did not love, whatever the prudence of it.
options.B: A sensible, well-provided match matters more than warmth of affection.

question: Choose A or B — whichever you would more naturally agree with.

options.A: I am content to follow my inclination of the moment rather than a fixed plan.
options.B: I prefer matters settled, ordered, and decided in good time.

question: Choose A or B — whichever you would more naturally agree with.

options.A: I welcome the unexpected and adapt my course readily.
options.B: I dislike surprises and like to know beforehand what is to come.
